title: Step 1: Toggle the button next to  to .

sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
To manage LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) users or groups, you must have
[Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) or [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) access to Atlas.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
You must meet the following prerequisites to manage user authentication
and authorization using LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) in Atlas:

- Atlas cluster using MongoDB 7.0 or later.

- LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) server using TLS (Transport Layer Security) that your Atlas clusters can access
  over the network using either  VPC or
  VNet peering connection or the cluster nodes' public IP addresses.

- LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) group memberships embedded as an attribute for each user in
  the LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) entry for user authorization only.

sections.heading: Recommendation
sections.body:
For your LDAPS (Secure Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) service to access Atlas clusters, MongoDB
recommends one of two configurations:

Using a VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) or VNet:

- Run your LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) server in a VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) or VNet.

- Establish a peering connection to your Atlas project.

- Use a public FQDN (fully qualified domain name) that resolves to the private IP address
  of your LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) server.

Using your data center:

- Run your LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) server with a public FQDN (fully qualified domain name) that resolves to a
  public IP address.

- Configure the LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) server to allow inbound access from the
  Atlas cluster nodes' public IP addresses.

sections.heading: Considerations
sections.body:
When you enable LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authentication and authorization, consider the
following:

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.body:
You cannot use both LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) and SCRAM authentication for the same
database user.

You cannot use OIDC (OpenID Connect) authentication when LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization is enabled.
When you enable LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization, the LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) server handles all
authorization decisions, including for any external authentication mechanisms.
Therefore, if you try to connect to Atlas by using OIDC (OpenID Connect),
the connection fails. You must disable LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization in order to use OIDC (OpenID Connect)
authentication.

sections.heading: Conflicts between LDAP Authorization and X.509 Users
sections.body:
If you enable LDAP authorization, you can't connect to your
clusters with users that authenticate with an
Atlas-managed X.509 certificate.

After you enable LDAP authorization, you *can* connect to your
clusters with users that authenticate with an
[self-managed X.509 certificate](self-managed-x509). However,
the user's Common Name in their X.509 certificate must match the
Distinguished Name of a user who is authorized to access your
database with LDAP.

sections.heading: Usernames
sections.body:
Atlas uses the full Distinguished Name (DN) of users in your LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol)
server as the Atlas username. For example, an example LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) user
named `ralph` has the following username in Atlas:

`cn=ralph,cn=Users,dc=aws-atlas-ldap-01,dc=myteam,dc=com`

sections.heading: Connection String
sections.body:
If the administrator enables user authentication or both user
authentication and authorization with LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol), database users must
override the following parameters in the connection string for their
clients.

- `authSource` must be `$external`

- `authenticationMechanism` must be `PLAIN`

> **Example:** The following connection string for [mongosh](mongodb-shell)
> authenticates an LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) user named `rob`:
>
> ```sh
> mongosh "mongodb+srv://cluster0-tijis.mongodb.net/test?authSource=%24external" \
>   --authenticationMechanism PLAIN \
>   --username cn=rob,cn=Users,dc=ldaps-01,dc=myteam,dc=com
> ```

To copy the connection string:

sections.heading: Step 1: Locate the connection string.
sections.body: Click **Connect**.

sections.heading: Step 2: Edit the connection string.
sections.body:
Edit the string with your `User DN` and password.

> **Note:** If your passwords, database names, or connection strings contain
> reserved URI characters, you must escape the characters. For example,
> if your password is `@bc123`, you must escape the `@`
> character when specifying the password in the connection
> string, such as `%40bc123`. To learn more, see [Special characters in connection string password](special-pass-characters).

sections.heading: Using Public IP Addresses
sections.body:
You can use public IP addresses that refer to other internal or private
IP addresses using Network Address Translation to allow Atlas
traffic to your LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) server. If you do this, be aware that
certain activities trigger a change
in the Atlas cluster's public IP addresses.

If you allowed LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) server access based on public IP addresses,
changes to the Atlas cluster's public IP address prevent LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol)
access. To restore LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) access, add the new Atlas cluster public
IP addresses to the LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) access list.

sections.heading: Configure Authentication with LDAP
sections.body:
Atlas CLI

To save one LDAP configuration for the project you specify using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas security ldap save [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas security ldap save](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-security-ldap-save).

Atlas UI

sections.heading: Configure Authentication with LDAP
sections.body: > **Note:** You can use the same Atlas CLI command to configure LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authentication and LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization.

sections.heading: Configure Authentication with LDAP
sections.body:
Use the following procedure to configure user authentication with
LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) for all clusters in a project.

> **Note:** You might incur additional costs when you enable this feature.
> See [Advanced Security](advanced-security).

You may list multiple servers separated by commas. You cannot use different ports.

You may provide self-signed certificates.

Wait for Atlas to deploy your changes. Atlas verifies that
your clusters can connect to, authenticate with, and query your LDAP
servers using the configuration details that you provided.

sections.heading: Configure Authorization
sections.body:
Atlas CLI

To save one LDAP configuration for the project you specify using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas security ldap save [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas security ldap save](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-security-ldap-save).

Atlas UI

sections.heading: Configure Authorization
sections.body:
> **Note:** You can use the same Atlas CLI command to configure LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol)
> authentication and LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization.

sections.heading: Configure Authorization
sections.body:
Use the following procedure to configure user authorization with
LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) for all clusters in a project.

> **Important:** 
>
> - You must enable authentication with LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) before enabling
>   authorization.
>
> - When you enable and configure LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization, database users
>   who are only configured for LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authentication will no longer
>   be able to access databases.

When a user attempts to perform an action, Atlas executes the
[LDAP query template](manual/reference/configuration-options)
to obtain the LDAP groups to which the authenticated user belongs.
Atlas permits the action if the query returns at least one group
that is authorized to perform the action. Atlas does not permit
the action if the query returns no groups that are authorized to
perform the action.

Atlas substitutes the authenticated username in the `{USER}`
placeholder when it runs the query. The query is relative to the host
specified in Server Hostname.

The formatting for the query must conform to [RFC4515](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4515).

If you do not provide a query template, Atlas applies the default
value: `{USER}?memberOf?base`.

Wait for Atlas to deploy your changes. Atlas verifies that
your clusters can connect to, authenticate with, and query your LDAP
server using the configuration details that you provide.

sections.heading: Add an LDAP Database User or Group
sections.body:
Atlas CLI

To create a database user for your project using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas dbusers create [builtInRole]... [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dbusers create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create).

Atlas UI

sections.heading: Add an LDAP Database User or Group
sections.body:
After you [configure authorization with LDAP](config-auth-ldap), follow these steps to create an LDAP
database user or group:

- If it isn't already displayed, click the
  Database Users tab.

- Click  Add New Database User.

In the Authentication Method section of the Add
New Database User modal window, select the box labeled LDAP.

> **Note:** If you don't see an LDAP option, you must
> [configure authorization with LDAP](config-auth-ldap).

Under LDAP Type, select LDAP User for a single
user or LDAP Group for an LDAP group.

Enter the authentication string for the LDAP user or LDAP group.

> **Example:** For the LDAP user named myUser in the project named projectName,
> enter the following:
>
> `cn=myUser,ou=projectName,dc=com`

If you enable LDAP authorization, you can create LDAP users, but they
can't access your clusters. Add an LDAP group to access
clusters with LDAP authorization enabled.

Select the database user privileges. You can assign privileges to the new user
in one or more of the following ways:

- Select a [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles) from the
  Built-in Role dropdown menu. You can select one
  built-in role per database user within the Atlas UI. If you delete the
  default option, you can click Add Built-in Role to select a new built-in role.

- If you have any [custom roles](mongodb-roles) defined, you can expand
  the Custom Roles section and select
  one or more roles from the Custom Roles dropdown menu. Click
  Add Custom Role to add more custom roles. You can also
  click the Custom Roles link to see the custom
  roles for your project.

- Expand the Specific Privileges section and select one or more
  [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles) from the
  Specific Privileges dropdown menu. Click
  Add Specific Privilege to add more privileges. This assigns the
  user specific privileges on individual databases and collections.

Atlas can apply a built-in role, multiple custom roles, and multiple specific
privileges to a single database user.

To remove an applied role or privilege, click 
Delete next to the role or privilege you wish to delete.

> **Note:** Atlas doesn't display the  Delete icon
> next to your Built-in Role, Custom Role, or
> Specific Privilege selection if you selected only one option. You
> can delete the selected role or privilege once you apply another role or privilege.

For more information on authorization, see [Role-Based
Access Control](manual/core/authorization) and [Built-in
Roles](manual/reference/built-in-roles) in the MongoDB manual.

By default, users can access all the clusters and federated database instances in the
project. You can restrict access to specific clusters and federated database instances
by doing the following:

- Toggle Restrict Access to Specific Clusters/Federated
  Database Instances to ON.

- Select the clusters and federated database instances to grant the user access to
  from the Grant Access To list.

* Toggle Temporary User or Temporary Group
  to On.

* Choose a time after which Atlas can delete the
  user or group from the Temporary User Duration or
  Temporary Group Duration dropdown. You can select one
  of the following time periods for the user or group to exist:

  - 6 hours

  - 1 day

  - 1 week

In the Database Users tab, temporary users and groups
display the time remaining until Atlas will delete the user or
group. Once Atlas deletes the user or group, any client or
application that uses the temporary user's or group's credentials
loses access to the cluster.

sections.heading: View LDAP Configuration
sections.body:
Atlas CLI

To return the details for one LDAP configuration using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas security ldap get [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas security ldap get](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-security-ldap-get).

Atlas UI

sections.heading: View LDAP Configuration
sections.body:
To view your current LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) settings using the Atlas UI:

Atlas displays your LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authentication settings.

To view your LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) *authorization* settings, toggle the button next to LDAP Authorization to On. Atlas displays your LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization settings.

sections.heading: Disable LDAP Configuration
sections.body:
Atlas CLI

To delete one LDAP configuration using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas security ldap delete [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas security ldap delete](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-security-ldap-delete).

Atlas UI

sections.heading: Disable LDAP Configuration
Disable LDAP Configuration
sections.body:
To disable your current LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) settings using the Atlas UI:

Atlas disables your LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authentication settings and your
LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization settings.

To disable LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization only, toggle the button next to LDAP Authorization to OFF. Atlas disables your LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization settings.

sections.heading: Tutorials for Third-Party LDAP Providers
sections.body:
Use the following tutorials to configure Atlas to authenticate and
authorize users from third-party LDAP providers:

- Set Up Authentication and Authorization with Entra Domain Services

- Configure User Authentication and Authorization with Okta LDAP Interface

- Configure User Authentication and Authorization with OneLogin VLDAP
